channel.title: Abstract data type on OOFP 2025-26
channel.description: Recent content in Abstract data type on OOFP 2025-26

item.title: ADT as a generic interface
item.description:
ADT as a generic interface #

An ADT in Java is usually a generic type, meaning that it accepts one or several types as parameter(s).

Example. A list of integers has type List<Integer>.

Syntax. The type List<E> is a generic type, with type variable E

Note. List<E> in Java is analogous to [a] in Haskell.

Constraint. In Java, a primitive type (like int or bool) cannot be a type parameter, whereas a boxed type (like Integer or Boolean) can.

item.title: List
item.description:
List #

The abstract data type list simulates a finite list.

Accordingly, a list may contain duplicates.

A list may expose the following methods:

  • “isEmpty” is self-explanatory,
  • “append” adds an element at the end of the list,
  • “prepend” adds an element at the beginning of the list,
  • “tail” returns the sub-list identical to the current one, but without its first element,
  • etc.

Note. The length of a list is usually unbounded (as opposed to the length of an array). This can be implemented in different ways, for instance with a dynamic array or a linked list.

item.title: Set
item.description:
Set #

The abstract data type set simulates a (usually finite) set.

A set may expose the following methods:

  • “add”: adds an element to the set if it was not present already (and returns true iff this was the case),
  • “contains”: checks whether an element belongs to the set,
  • “size”: returns the cardinality of the set,
  • etc.

Warning. By default, there is no guarantee on the order of elements in a set.

item.title: Associative array
item.description:
Associative array #

An associative array (or dictionary or map) simulates a function with finite domain.

Equivalently, it can viewed as a finite set of key-value pairs, where keys are distinct.

Example. The following associative array maps a city to its country:

$\qquad \{ \textit{Verona} \mapsto \textit{Italy},\ \textit{Rome} \mapsto \textit{Italy},\ \textit{Innsbruck} \mapsto \textit{Austria} \}$

The keys are $\textit{Verona}, \textit{Rome}$ and $\textit{Innsbruck}$.
The values are $\textit{Italy}$ and $\textit{Austria}$.

An associative array exposes at least the following methods:

item.title: Stack, queue, priority queue
item.description:
Stack, queue, priority queue #

Some abstract data types impose strong limitations on the operations that they allow. These limitations provide opportunities for specialized implementations (i.e. specialized data structures), which can be very efficient in some contexts.

Stack #

A stack (or Last In First Out queue or LIFO queue) simulates a collection organized as a physical stack (for instance a stack of plates).

A stack exposes three main methods:
